column10: As a first-generation student myself, I resonated with the mission of SpeakHire to help these students to gain cultural and social capital. This is something that means a great deal to me as I became self-aware regarding my own story through my master's degree in Educational Leadership. I learned a great deal about first-gen students and cultural capital and the role it can play in helping you achieve social mobility. As someone that had to grind my way through education, I appreciate every person that helped me along the way. We never do everything alone. There are always those people who cared enough to help. So I am glad I could have the opportunity to do the same for someone else.

column119:
Most of my interns (except Pamela) would've been better off with a Champion that's fluent in Spanish. I think the others got maybe 60% of what I said.

I don't think interns can get much out of this program if they're commuting. That limits who can participate, but I'd emphasize quality over quantity. The Intern who was on the bus during our sessions couldn't participate much because of the background noise, and couldn't hear me sometimes. Those communication challenges slowed down the session for the others too, and put the onus on others to carry more of the conversation.

I think it would've been easier to engage and connect with 1 committed Intern vs. 4. I'm torn because I know you intentionally group multiple Interns for good reasons (e.g. can keep the session if 1 doesn't show up). On the other hand, it created more of a classroom setting and was hard to give really personalized attention and build deeper connections.

I would've loved to get notes and recommendations from the previous Champion(s).

A learning for me is these Interns were probably too young for career skills, and if I did this again, I'd spend a LOT more time just getting to know them and their challenges, helping them develop self awareness and learn what their interests and strengths are, and storytelling.
column120:
My interns are great. I can tell they're smart, good kids. And I'm impressed by the commitment to showing up from some of them, e.g. Wilman usually calls in even though he's on the bus during the regular session time.

Everyone at Speakhire has been helpful and responsive.

The training we received as Champions was spot-on in terms of what to expect. My first session was pretty difficult, but would've been more disheartening if you all hadn't prepared me for little engagement, cameras off, language barrier, etc.

column119: Have a more rigorous application process for interns. Interns should have a clear understanding of what the internship entails (what its purpose is, full commitment to attending all sessions, etc.). Interns should be able to articulate why they want to complete an internship and what their goals are for the internship. Interns should be coached on appropriate ways to communicate with their champions and actually respond to emails/text/etc. There should be more consequences for failing to attend sessions and make-up sessions. There could also be improvements made to the matching process. Champions should only be matched with students whose goals and career interests closely align with the champion. If this means champions have to wait a few rounds before being matched, it would be better than having a bad match that ruins the entire process. If possible, it would also be helpful if SPEAKHIRE hired more staff that speak multiple languages. Multi-lingual SPEAKHIRE employees (or volunteers) could then jump in on calls to help translate and reduce the language barrier, rather than putting the emphasis on finding champions that are multi-lingual in addition to being in a career of interest.

column119: 1) Extend time for each session? One feedback I had previously is having 2 interns in one 45 minute session is quite challenging. Probably 45mins/session for 1 intern and 60mins/session for 2 interns would be more efficient, so they both have a chance to speak up; 2) Clarify expectations from interns - Currently champions have a freedom to choose skills/topics for each session. However, I think it would be more effective for interns if they know (and share) what they want to learn from their champions as well. The ideal situation is a match between an intern who wants to follow the same career of champions, but I can imagine it is not always the case. I asked interns if anything specific they want to know during the sessions too, but it is also good and helpful with champions to know in advance to customize the sessions to interns' need
column120: SpeakHire is a great platform for career orientation and preparation! By connecting young students and professionals, SpeakHire enables knowledge sharing, skills transferring and motivation boost. For students, joining SpeakHire can help clarify their career paths and better prepare for the next step of their study or career. For professionals, it is a great way to give back to society, together with reflect our career and sharpen our mentoring and training skills. I am proud to be part of this journey, and look forward to make more impacts on supporting young immigrant students with SpeakHire.

column27: I told both my interns they could e-mail me in the future to talk about college/ internships, etc. Since I am on the West Coast and have lived in LA, San Francisco, and Seattle, I told them they could contact me if they have any interest in those regions.
column119:
1) Not sure if we can obtain this information: it would be helpful to know the mentee's parents' backgrounds/ occupations ahead of time. I could ask during a session but it might be embarrassing for some mentees.

One advantage I had despite being a refugee/ poor/ raised by a single parent is my mother was reasonably well-educated (finished high school, worked in an urban hospital/ bank) in her original country. So although she could not help me with English/ US culture much, she placed a high value on education, chose homes near libraries, and taught me early on skills like how to prepare for an work interview. 

I had a friend who was Spanish interpreter: she told me that some patients/ clients she worked with not only had problems with English but with Spanish. Their first language might be an indigenous dialect of Mexico and some from rural areas only had a 3rd grade or less education in Spanish. A mentee with that type of background may face more challenges than a mentee whose dad now works as a janitor but was formerly a lawyer in their native country.

2) The restrictions on "homework" could be loosened. I can understand we don't want to burden our mentees further but I felt a bit frustrated that asking them for 30 minutes or an hour of time to look up/ learn more information after the sessions was discouraged. I think we should encourage them to learn to look up info on their own as well as this is an important skill to have. 

3) It would be good to know of other ongoing efforts at the mentees' schools, e.g. are colleges coming out to talk to them, do they have 'Career Talks', etc.? For the latter, I think it's important for mentees to hear about a variety of careers before choosing/ deciding on one. 
Narrowing too early can be a problem although I can well understand the pressure - from themselves, parents, society, teachers, etc. -  on mentees to do so. In high school and early college, the goal should be to have a well-rounded education. That opens up more opportunities: I was a science student who took enough liberal arts classes (history, literature, linguistics, Latin, etc.) to qualify for my school's liberal arts honors society. To this day, my science colleagues value me for my communication skills.

One of the most memorable experiences I had in college was a statistics professor who reserved every Friday to have someone from outside the university come in and talk to us about how they used statistics in their work. Although this was a specific subject, it helped to see historians, economists, medical researchers, lawyers, businessman, etc. talk about their daily work. That professor was beloved by the students because he made his classes relevant to real life.
